title: Leviathan
subtitle: Or The Matter, Forme and Power of a Commonwealth Ecclesiasticall and Civil
author: Thomas Hobbes
excerpt: The foundational text of social contract theory, arguing that rational self-interest compels individuals to submit to an absolute sovereign to escape the state of nature — 'solitary, poor, nasty, brutish, and short.'
keyIdeas: State of nature as war of all against all
Fear of violent death as the foundation of political obligation
Laws of nature are theorems of reason, not enforceable commands
The sovereign as artificial person representing the multitude
Ecclesiastical authority must be subordinate to civil authority
keyTakeaways: Life without government is a state of war driven by competition, diffidence, and glory
The only rational escape from this condition is a covenant establishing absolute sovereignty
The sovereign is not party to the contract and cannot be accused of injustice
Subjects retain only the right of self-defence when their lives are directly threatened
Religious authority must be subordinate to civil authority to prevent civil war
